A funding prospectus · 2018–2024
THE MANGO PROJECT KHARTOUM · NUBA

The Sudanese mango, ready for the world.

A proposal for a vapor-heat-treatment packing centre that unlocks Sudan's 600,000-ton, chemical-free mango harvest for the Gulf, European and Asian export markets — and turns a rural, war-displaced community into a sustainable agricultural economy.

600KtAnnual harvest potential
6Export-grade varieties

Abu Samaka, Shendi, Kent, Keitt, Tommy Atkins and Alphonse — harvested December through September.

§ 01
Who we are

We grow fruit, but we are really growing communities.

Risemeup Sustainable Farming is a non-profit agricultural initiative rebuilding rural livelihoods in Sudan — beginning with the communities of the Nuba Mountains, displaced by decades of conflict, and the 250-acre Virgin Mary Farm.

For three generations these farmers have grown some of the finest chemical-free mango on earth. But without a packing centre certified for international quarantine standards, up to 90 % of that fruit is lost to fruit fly, and the rest sells at a fraction of its value on the domestic market.

The Mango Project closes that gap. One modern facility, built around a Japanese vapor-heat-treatment system, turns a neglected orchard economy into an export-grade agribusiness — and turns a displaced community into one that employs, trains and feeds itself.

Nuba Mountain community

Displaced from their home land by the former regime, this rural community of farmers is the project's first beneficiary — through training, employment and equity in the co-operative structure.

Virgin Mary Farm · 250 acres

The anchor site. Irrigated, planted with export varieties, and earmarked for both the packing centre and a downstream processing line for off-grade fruit (drying, peeling, cutting).

§ 02
The country, the context

A land built on agriculture, waiting for the equipment to match its soil.

1.9M
km² of land
Sudan is the third-largest country in Africa. Horticulture is a historic strength.
67%
Live rurally
Of 35.5 million Sudanese, two in three live in rural areas where poverty reaches 58 %.
70–80%
Rural labour
Agriculture employs the overwhelming majority of the rural workforce (World Bank).
¾
Lost oil wealth
Since South Sudan's independence in 2011. Non-oil exports — mango, foremost — are the country's future.
"Rural development is central to ending hunger and extreme poverty. Investing in rural community is a long-term solution to so many of the problems we face today — hunger, poverty, youth unemployment and forced migration all have deep roots in rural areas." — Risemeup founding brief, 2018
§ 03
Why mango, why now

Think globally, act locally.

Sudan produces mango at a scale most countries can only dream of — and almost no one knows it.

The fruit grows across the country, with a production window stretching from December to late September: a nine-month season that alone outperforms every major mango nation in the world. Six varieties — Abu Samaka, Alphonse, Shendi, Kent, Keitt and Tommy Atkins — are commercially suited for international export. All of it grows without synthetic inputs. It is, by any modern standard, an organic product.

100 % chemical-free

Sudanese mango is grown without synthetic pesticides or fertilisers. In a market that pays a premium for organic provenance, this is the project's single greatest commercial asset.

Zero-input cultivation

Nine-month season

Harvest runs December through September, meaning the facility operates counter-seasonally to India, Mexico and the Philippines — and supplies Gulf and European buyers during their off-window.

Dec → Sep

Six export varieties

A portfolio that covers every segment — from the sweet, early Abu Samaka to the long-shelf-life Keitt and Tommy Atkins preferred by European supermarkets. Diversification built into the orchard.

Full-spectrum basket
§ 04
The harvest, varietal

Six fruits, one orchard.

Each variety fills a different slot on the global shelf — which is how a single packing centre can serve Gulf supermarkets, European discounters and Asian premium channels simultaneously.

Flagship

Abu Samaka

Indigenous · Sudan

Pale-green skin, saffron flesh, exceptional aroma. The mango that built Sudan's reputation in the Gulf. Organic by default and available in quantity.

Jun – AugPeak harvest
GulfPrimary market
Classic

Tommy Atkins

Florida stock · grown in Sudan

The workhorse of the global supermarket trade: thick skin, long shelf life, excellent for surface and sea freight. Perfect for European discounters.

Jul – SepPeak harvest
EUPrimary market
Premium

Kent

Premium variety

Rich, nearly fibreless flesh. The variety of choice for European premium retail and speciality grocery. Commands a meaningful price premium per kilo.

Jun – AugPeak harvest
EU / AsiaPrimary market
Shelf life

Keitt

Late-season · high yield

Large, oblong, yellow-green skin. Late-season variety that extends the harvest window and has the longest transport tolerance of the portfolio.

Aug – SepPeak harvest
Gulf / EUPrimary market
Historic

Alphonse

South-Asian heritage

The "king of mangoes". Sweetness, aroma, honeyed flesh. The premium Asian diaspora market pays extraordinary prices for certified Alphonse.

May – JulPeak harvest
Asia / UKPrimary market
Regional

Shendi

Northern Sudan

Local-favourite variety from the Shendi region — sweet, juicy, best eaten fresh. A regional identity product that differentiates the brand in-market.

May – JulPeak harvest
GulfPrimary market
§ 05
The bottleneck

One insect is blocking an entire industry.

Bactrocera dorsalis — the fruit fly. It attacks the ripening mango, lays eggs under the skin, and the larvae tunnel into the flesh. By the time anyone can see it, the fruit is lost.

Every serious import market in the world — the Gulf, the EU, the UK, Japan, Korea — demands certified phytosanitary quarantine treatment to guarantee no fruit-fly load enters their supply chain. Without it, Sudan's mangoes are stopped at the border.

There is no modern vapor-heat-treatment facility operating in Sudan today. That single missing link is why a country capable of producing 600,000 tons a year sells almost all of it into a depressed domestic market at cents per kilo.

90%
Crop loss to fruit-fly damage without treatment
§ 06
The solution · main facility

Vapor Heat Treatment. Model VHC-250M.

FTH CO., LTD · KAGOSHIMA · JAPAN

A differential-pressure chamber that kills fruit-fly eggs at 47 °C in twenty minutes — then cools and dries the fruit ready for shipment.

The Japanese VHT is the international gold standard, certified across Asian and Gulf markets.
i.
Treatment capacity
2,500 kg / batch

Seven-layer pallet architecture. 252 baskets at 10 units, 12 kg per basket.

ii.
Treatment cycle
3½ hours

Automatically operated, touch-panel controlled, quarantine-officer witnessed.

iii.
Kill temperature
47 °C

Held for 20+ minutes at 90 % RH — the Thailand domestic mango quarantine standard.

iv.
Capital cost (FOB)
¥40M JPY

Including installation and trial run in Sudan. Quotation from FTH Co., Ltd., 28 March 2018.

The treatment cycle, step by step

01
Load
Fruit into processing containers, containers into the chamber.
02
Sensor set
Core-temperature probes placed, touch-panel run started.
03
43 °C
Raise mango core temperature at a controlled rate, 55 % RH.
04
47 °C · 20 min
Sterilise. Saturated vapor heat, 90 %+ RH — the critical kill step.
05
Cool & dry
Shower-cooling followed by a dry-heat control pass.
06
Certify
Quarantine officer signs off. Certificate issued. Fruit moves to cold storage.
§ 07
The packing centre, end to end

From the truck to the ship, under one roof.

§ 08
Who runs this

A founder, a professor, a farm.

Founder

Iman Tadros

Founder of Risemeup Sustainable Farming. Architect of the Virgin Mary Farm project, convener of the Nuba Mountain community partnership, and the project's outward face to investors, buyers and development institutions.

Risemeup Sustainable Farming
Head of Mango Project Development

Prof. (PhD) Elamin Ali Ahmed

Lead agronomist for the packing centre, authored the feasibility study on the VHT machine, and liaison with FTH Co., Ltd. in Kagoshima for the equipment procurement.

Fund the machine. We will deliver the orchard.

Risemeup is seeking capital partners — philanthropic, institutional, or strategic — to co-finance the VHT system and the packing-centre build-out. The quotation is in hand, the orchard exists, the team is in place, and the market is paying triple the domestic price for certified fruit. The only missing piece is the equipment on the floor.
